factors.name: Market Excess Return (MKT)
factors.description: The excess return of the market portfolio over the risk-free rate, representing systematic market risk premium

l1.definition: Market excess return is the return difference between the broad market index and the risk-free rate, capturing systematic market risk premium

param_constraints.market_proxy: Must be broad market index covering NYSE/AMEX/NASDAQ

l1.business_constraints: Used as benchmark factor in all three-factor model applications; cannot be isolated for trading without hedging other exposures

calculation_steps.formula: Rm_t = Σ(w_i × R_i_t) where w_i = MV_i / ΣMV_i

calculation_steps.description: Obtain risk-free rate from Treasury bills

l2.edge_case_handling: Use stale prices for suspended stocks; holidays aligned with CRSP calendar
l2.missing_value_handling: Interpolate if T-bill data missing; use prior month if necessary
l2.complexity: O(N) for N stocks in universe

l3.financial_intuition: Investors require compensation for bearing systematic market risk that cannot be diversified away. The market risk premium reflects aggregate risk aversion and economic uncertainty
l3.market_behavior: MKT loads on beta > 0 for all stocks; high-beta portfolios load more on MKT. During market downturns, MKT is negative and high-beta stocks underperform more
l3.theoretical_basis: CAPM predicts all cross-sectional variation in expected returns is explained by market beta. MKT is the sole priced risk factor in CAPM
l3.historical_effectiveness: Average MKT premium of 4.8% annualized (1963-1991) with t-statistic > 3.0. Explains approximately 70% of individual stock return variation
l3.related_factors: Foundation for SMB and HML factors; interaction with value and size factors creates the three-factor model

hypotheses.name: Market Beta Pricing
hypotheses.description: Higher market beta should command higher expected returns in equilibrium

hypotheses.name: Systematic Risk Dominance
hypotheses.description: Market risk is the primary source of cross-sectional return variation

l4.meaning_summary: MKT measures the reward for bearing aggregate market risk; foundational factor that explains most but not all cross-sectional return variation
key_insights: Market beta alone under-explains returns for small-cap and value stocks
MKT premium is time-varying and positively correlated with volatility
MKT is orthogonal to size and value factors by construction
l4.uncertainty: Market risk premium is not stable over time; equity premium puzzle remains unresolved

factors.name: SMB (Small Minus Big)
factors.description: Return spread between small-capitalization and large-capitalization stocks, capturing size-based risk premium

l1.definition: SMB is the average return on three small-stock portfolios minus the average return on three big-stock portfolios, isolating the size effect

param_constraints.size_threshold: Median-based split (50th percentile) is standard; other splits may be used
param_constraints.breakpoints: Must use NYSE breakpoints for comparability with academic literature

l1.business_constraints: Size sorting requires market cap data; must account for listing exchange differences. SMB is zero-investment portfolio by construction

calculation_steps.description: Sort all stocks by market cap at June-end; split at NYSE median
calculation_steps.formula: Small = stocks with MV < NYSE_median_MV; Big = stocks with MV >= NYSE_median_MV

calculation_steps.description: Form 6 value-weighted portfolios from 2 × 3 sorts (size × B/M)
calculation_steps.formula: S/L, S/M, S/H, B/L, B/M, B/H

calculation_steps.description: Calculate monthly returns for each portfolio
Compute SMB as average small minus average big

l2.edge_case_handling: IPO stocks require minimum 6 months of data; delisted stocks use CRSP delisting return; REITs excluded
l2.missing_value_handling: Exclude stocks with missing market cap; require 24 months of returns for portfolio inclusion
l2.complexity: O(T × N) for time-series of portfolio calculations

l3.financial_intuition: Small firms are riskier due to lower liquidity, higher financial distress probability, and less analyst coverage. Investors demand premium for bearing this residual risk not captured by market beta
l3.market_behavior: Small stocks underperform during economic contractions and credit crunches; outperform during economic recoveries. High beta but also high idiosyncratic volatility
l3.theoretical_basis: Banz (1981) discovered size effect; interpreted as either risk premium (distress costs) or market inefficiency (limited attention, information asymmetry)
l3.historical_effectiveness: SMB premium of 3.0% annualized (1963-1991) with t-statistic ~2.8. Premium has weakened post-1990s due to increased arbitrage
l3.related_factors: Negatively correlated with HML (~-0.3); value stocks tend to be small, creating confound. Independent once controlling for B/M

hypotheses.name: Size Risk Premium
hypotheses.description: Small-cap stocks earn higher returns than large-cap stocks after controlling for market beta

hypotheses.name: Financial Distress Channel
hypotheses.description: Small firms face higher probability of financial distress, commanding risk premium

hypotheses.name: Liquidity Premium
hypotheses.description: Small stocks have lower liquidity, requiring compensation for trading costs

l4.meaning_summary: SMB captures systematic risk related to firm size that is not explained by market beta alone; premium varies with economic conditions
key_insights: Size effect is robust to market beta controls but not to value controls
SMB premium concentrated in micro-cap stocks (bottom 10%)
Premium has largely disappeared in post-publication period (post-1993)
l4.uncertainty: Whether SMB represents true risk premium or behavioral anomaly remains debated. Factor decay suggests arbitrage has largely eliminated the anomaly

factors.name: HML (High Minus Low)
factors.description: Return spread between high book-to-market (value) stocks and low book-to-market (growth) stocks, capturing value premium

l1.definition: HML is the average return on two high B/M (value) portfolios minus the average return on two low B/M (growth) portfolios, isolating the value effect

default_params.book_value_source: Compustat: book equity = total assets - total liabilities - preferred stock + deferred taxes
default_params.fiscal_year_lag: Use fiscal year-end B/M from prior year (minimum 6-month lag)

param_constraints.bm_breakpoints: Must use NYSE breakpoints; bottom 30% = low, top 30% = high
param_constraints.book_value_lag: Minimum 6 months between fiscal year-end and portfolio formation to avoid look-ahead bias

l1.business_constraints: Book value must be positive; negative B/M stocks excluded from value sort. B/M calculated as fiscal year-end book equity / June market cap

calculation_steps.description: Calculate B/M ratio using Compustat book equity and June-end market cap
Sort stocks into B/M thirds at NYSE breakpoints (30th, 70th percentile)
calculation_steps.formula: Low_BM = bottom 30%, Mid_BM = middle 40%, High_BM = top 30%

calculation_steps.description: Form 6 value-weighted portfolios from 2 × 3 sorts (size × B/M)
calculation_steps.formula: S/L, S/M, S/H, B/L, B/M, B/H

calculation_steps.description: Calculate HML as average high B/M minus average low B/M

l2.edge_case_handling: Exclude financial firms (SIC 6000-6999) due to leverage normalization issues; negative book equity firms placed in bottom B/M decile
l2.missing_value_handling: Require 12 months of prior returns; missing book equity handled with cross-sectional imputation from industry peers
l2.complexity: O(T × N) for time-series of portfolio calculations

l3.financial_intuition: High B/M firms are financially distressed, out-of-favor, or restructuring. They carry higher fundamental risk (earnings distress, analyst neglect) that is not captured by market beta. Low B/M (growth) firms have bright prospects but are priced optimistically
l3.market_behavior: Value stocks outperform during economic recoveries and high-inflation regimes; growth stocks outperform during speculative bubbles and low-interest-rate environments. HML negatively correlated with market return direction
l3.theoretical_basis: Fama-French (1993) interpret HML as distress risk premium. Alternative: Lakonishok, Shleifer, Vishny (1994) argue HML reflects mispricing rather than risk
l3.historical_effectiveness: HML premium of 5.0% annualized (1963-1991) with t-statistic > 4.0. One of the most robust factor premiums in academic literature
l3.related_factors: Correlated with earnings quality (RMW), investment patterns (CMA). Value-growth cycle is opposite to momentum; HML negatively correlated with market at short horizons

hypotheses.name: Value Risk Premium
hypotheses.description: High book-to-market (value) stocks earn higher returns than low book-to-market (growth) stocks after controlling for market beta

hypotheses.name: Distress Risk Channel
hypotheses.description: Value firms face higher probability of financial distress or business decline, requiring risk premium

hypotheses.name: Cash Flow Duration
hypotheses.description: Growth stocks have longer cash flow duration; discount rate changes affect them more, creating negative HML-beta correlation

l4.meaning_summary: HML captures systematic risk related to fundamental value/growth characteristics; value premium reflects either distress risk compensation or investor overreaction to bad news
key_insights: HML is the strongest factor in the three-factor model with highest t-statistic
Value premium concentrated in small-cap value stocks (S/H portfolio)
HML premium has weakened substantially post-2007, with growth outperforming during 2010-2020
l4.uncertainty: Whether HML represents true risk premium or market inefficiency is unresolved. Post-2007 factor decay raises concerns about arbitrage-based explanations

factors.name: Fama-French Three-Factor Model Portfolio
factors.description: Combined long-short portfolio implementing the full three-factor model: long value-growth spread (HML) and size spread (SMB) while neutralizing market exposure
factors.formula: R_i,t - Rf,t = α_i + β_i MKT_t + s_i SMB_t + h_i HML_t + ε_i,t

l1.definition: Combined factor portfolio that implements long-short strategies based on both size and value characteristics, capturing premiums from both SMB and HML simultaneously

default_params.formation_month: June (using prior fiscal year book equity)

param_constraints.universe: Exclude REITs, ADRs, stocks with price < $5
param_constraints.look_ahead: Book equity must be lagged minimum 6 months from fiscal year-end

l1.business_constraints: Factor construction requires simultaneous access to CRSP and Compustat; factors available for download from French Data Library. Transaction costs from annual rebalancing must be considered

calculation_steps.description: Obtain June-end market cap and prior fiscal year-end book equity for all stocks
Sort stocks by size (median NYSE) and B/M (30th/70th NYSE percentiles)
calculation_steps.formula: 6 portfolios: S/L, S/M, S/H, B/L, B/M, B/H

calculation_steps.description: Calculate monthly value-weighted returns for each portfolio

calculation_steps.formula: SMB = avg(S/L,S/M,S/H) - avg(B/L,B/M,B/H); HML = avg(S/H,B/H) - avg(S/L,B/L)

calculation_steps.description: Form long-short trading portfolio (e.g., long S/H, short B/L)

l2.edge_case_handling: Delisted stocks use CRSP delisting return; M&A survivors use surviving firm returns; stock splits adjusted via CRSP adjustment factors
l2.missing_value_handling: Exclude stocks with < 24 months of returns; missing book equity replaced with industry median
l2.complexity: O(T × N × K) where K is number of portfolios (6)

l3.financial_intuition: The combined factor portfolio exploits systematic return patterns related to firm characteristics (size and value) that are not explained by market beta alone. Long small-cap value, short large-cap growth captures both size and value premiums simultaneously
l3.market_behavior: Factor portfolio earns positive returns when value stocks outperform growth and small stocks outperform large. Correlation with market is low by construction (factors are orthogonalized). Performance peaks during economic recoveries and value up-cycles
l3.theoretical_basis: Fama-French (1993) demonstrate that MKT, SMB, and HML jointly explain 90%+ of diversified portfolio variation. Combined factors explain cross-sectional returns better than CAPM alone
l3.historical_effectiveness: Combined S/H - B/L portfolio earned approximately 8-10% annualized excess return (1963-1991). R² of factor model ~ 0.90 vs. ~ 0.70 for CAPM. GRS test rejects CAPM at 1% level
l3.related_factors: Foundation for Fama-French Five-Factor Model (adds RMW, CMA). Basis for Carhart Four-Factor Model (adds momentum). Extends to quality-factor and profitability-factor models

hypotheses.name: Three-Factor Pricing
hypotheses.description: MKT, SMB, and HML jointly explain cross-sectional variation in expected stock returns

hypotheses.name: Factor Portfolio Alpha
hypotheses.description: Well-constructed factor portfolios (long SMB/HML, short opposite) should have alpha close to zero

hypotheses.name: Explanatory Power
hypotheses.description: Three-factor model explains significantly more return variation than CAPM alone

hypotheses.name: Bond Market Integration
hypotheses.description: Same three factors explain cross-sectional variation in bond returns

l4.meaning_summary: Combined factor portfolio implementing the Fama-French three-factor model, capturing systematic premiums from size and value characteristics while controlling for market risk
key_insights: Three factors explain ~90% of diversified portfolio variance vs. ~70% for CAPM
Factor premiums are time-varying and regime-dependent
Factor decay is documented post-publication, suggesting arbitrage pressure
Model is foundational for modern factor investing and asset pricing research
l4.uncertainty: Whether factors represent risk premia or behavioral anomalies remains debated. Factor decay post-1993 raises questions about economic interpretation. Hundreds of proposed factors create 'factor zoo' problem

reasoning: The Fama-French 1993 paper introduces three core factors (MKT, SMB, HML) that collectively explain cross-sectional stock return variation. While MKT is the foundational market factor, SMB and HML are the novel contributions that extend CAPM. The combined three-factor model portfolio (long S/H, short B/L) represents the full implementation of the paper's methodology, capturing both size and value premiums. All factors map to 'factor_rank' signal type since the methodology involves sorting stocks by characteristics and forming long-short portfolios. The paper is foundational for modern quantitative factor investing with high confidence in historical effectiveness, though factor decay post-publication reduces forward-looking expectations.
